videos.transcript: the Allure of New Orleans the largest city in Louisiana lies in its status as a cultural hotspot brimming with a rich history and Soulful Jazz and Blues melodies positioned along the Mississippi River near the Gulf of Mexico New Orleans transcends the label of a mere picturesque destination with its museums parks and vibrant architecture as the City offers an immersive experience on and off the famed Bourbon Street whether you're exploring historical sites enjoying Lively street festivals or indulging in tantalizing flavors of Creole and Cajun cuisine New Orleans promises an unforgettable Adventure welcome to travel-minded today we're counting down the 13 best things to do while you're in New Orleans Louisiana are you a fan of our videos be sure to subscribe and ring the bell to be notified about our latest videos number 13 Oak Alley Plantation named for its enchanting double row of trees that warmly welcomes visitors visiting the Oak Alley Plantation is a must while you're in New Orleans Louisiana just an hour's Drive West of the city this Historic Site situated along the Mississippi River since its founding in 1837 is renowned for its attractive antibellum architecture and captivating grounds the Mansion showcases a delightful Greek Revival Style with 28 colossal freestanding columns the standout feature is the awe inspiring double row of 300-year-old oak trees that gracefully form a canopy over the driveway as you explore the estate you'll delve into its Rich history learn about the enslaved individuals who worked there and have ample opportunities to capture photos of the picturesque oakl Avenue and the historic house beyond the visual Delights visitors can enjoy a tour of the ground and Exhibits stay overnight at the Inn or indulge in a traditional Southern meal at the onsite Cafe noteworthy attractions include the slavery exhibit the sugarcan exhibit in theater a blacksmith shop and a civil war tent number 12 Street car ride with the regional transit authority street cars you can effortlessly explore every nook and cranny of Crescent City connecting diverse neighborhoods from Uptown to Downtown lake view to the lower nine and even spanning the Mississippi River to the parish lines these street cars offer a cost-effective journey through the heart of New Orleans as you travel enjoy the Serene views of the city's Beauty charm and Rich history among the various street car routes the St Charles Avenue Line Stands out as the world's oldest continuously operating Street Railway a 150y old tradition this route from the French quarter to the Garden District takes approximately 45 minutes each way for just a125 it provides a budget-friendly opportunity to Marvel at New Orleans architecture and visit iconic sites like udon Park to Lane and lyola University the St Charles Street car is more than just transportation it's a romantic Journey filled with Nostalgia showcasing classic style and picturesque scenes of historic architecture and Majestic oak trees number 11 tours known for its Rich history of voodoo and tales of Paranormal Activity some people have dubbed New Orleans the most haunted city in America the mustsee attractions include unique cemeteries with above ground tombs adorned with ornate French and Spanish inspired designs despite the Aged appearance of these burial grounds featuring crumbled tombs and patchy grass they add to the ghostly Ambiance that attracts top Ghost Tours St Louis cemeteries particularly St Louis Cemetery number one are popular stops on these tours housing the final resting places of intriguing figures like Bernard de maragy and Marie Lavo the Voodoo Queen New Orleans offers various walking tours including ghost vampire and Voodoo tours as well as attractions like the Mortuary Haunted House and the New Orleans Museum of death during Halloween the city also hosts several haunted house experiences number 10 the Garden District history and architecture come alive in the captivating Garden District of New Orleans just 3 miles from the vibrant French Quarter known for its lavish mansions and meticulously landscaped Gardens this District provides a serene escape from the Lively atmosphere of its neighboring French Quarter once serving as a cemetery for yellow fever victims the area is now adorned with sprawling oak trees and ample green spaces setting the stage for a laid-back Ambiance ideal for strolls you can take a walking tour to Marvel at The District's Exquisite homes after you are done and you need a delightful recharge head to the intersection of pronia street and Washington Avenue here you'll discover a Charming mix of shops cafes and the iconic Lafayette Cemetery number one adjacent to the cemetery Commander's Palace restaurant awaits standing in as one of the city's most esteemed and bustling dining establishments spaning from St Charles Avenue to Magazine Street and from Jackson Avenue to Louisiana Avenue the Garden District is easily accessible via the St Charles Avenue street car Magazine Street bus or Carriage immerse yourself in the rich history and tranquil beauty of this historic neighborhood number nine City Park almost like a microcosm of New Orleans itself City Park encapsulates the essence of the city's vibrant life within its sprawling 1,300 acre landscape bigger than Central Park in New York City this Urban Oasis is one of the United States oldest and largest parks with its expansive green spaces waterways and eclectic mix of attractions City Park caters to the tastes of nature lovers outdoor enthusiasts and curious Travelers alike among its notable highlights are the New Orleans Museum of Art and its sculpture garden the cuchery forest with eight distinct ecosystems housing labord Mountain the city's highest point and the expansive New Orleans botanical garden with 2,000 plant varieties families can enjoy the carousel gardens amusement park and the Louisiana children's museum at the same time adults can Savor a leisurely walk on the great Lawns before indulging in a delicious dinner at the esteemed Ralph's On The Park not just a recreational Haven city park is home to the world's largest Grove of mature Live Oaks including the awe inspiring anman Oak and McDon Oak both believed to be between 750 and 900 years old despite its grandor City Park remains off the tourist radar providing a tranquil Escape for those seeking a quiet place to spend a few delightful hours number eight local Cuisine perhaps more than anywhere else New Orleans is a city filled with iconic dishes that embody its vibrant culinary culture PoBoy jumbalaya gumbo and beet are culinary Treasures that resonate globally defining the city's unique identity dishes like the gumball or herbs at dookie Chase and the turtle soup at commanders contribute to a restaurant's history and shape The Narrative of New Orleans Cuisine locals passionately argue that gumbo is practically its food group when in New Orleans indulging in crawfish at tfet is a must and the well-known jumbalaya epitomizes the city's Fame for traditional rice dishes the world's first cocktail the Sazerac crafted by creel Apothecary Antoine pad in the mid 1800s is the official drink of New Orleans the sazar house a museum dedicated entirely to this iconic cocktail since its opening in 2019 explores its Origins and the city's Rich cocktail culture after a walking tour nothing beats a PoBoy whether filled with shrimp fried oysters or roast beef Parkway and dalees two of the city's best and most authentic spots offer a true Taste of New New Orleans originating at Central Grocery in the French Quarter the muetta a famous sandwich with ham salami provolone cheese and Olive dressing is another culinary gem lastly Char grilled oysters a menu Staple in The Big Easy are a must try at Felix's restaurant and Oyster Bar an institution with multiple locations throughout the city these local Cuisines are a must for you to try number seven swamp tour since swamps in Circle New Orleans a captivating tour through the synomous bou region of Southern Louisiana becomes an essential experience these wetlands are the lifeblood of the city and immersing yourself in a swamp tour is the best way to grasp the true essence of New Orleans Venture through expansive Landscapes soak in breathtaking scenery and become one with nature across the vast Wetlands numerous tour companies offer excursions to the swamps with the Jean lafit National Historic Park and preserve being a particularly beloved destination for an unconventional Skyline View take on the exhilarating challenge of a zipline tour through the marpa swamp alongside guided hikes and kayaking trips you can also opt for thrilling Airboat Tours offering a chance to spot alligators snakes Turtles and other captivating Louisiana Wildlife In The Mysterious Waters of the swamp number six Jackson Square One can't miss destination in New Orleans is Jackson Square nestled in the heart of the French Quarter surrounded by beautiful buildings such as the St Louis Cathedral and the cabildo and pontalba buildings the Historic Park marks where the famous Louisiana Purchase occurred recognized as one of the finest public spaces in the country it earned National Historic Landmark status in 1960 at its Center stands a striking statue of the seventh US President Andrew Jackson while Street artists musicians and fortune tellers contribute to the Lively Ambiance with over 2 million annual visitors Jackson Square hosts public events weddings and photo sessions and has been a backdrop for numerous movies and TV shows the park maintaining its original Parisian design features iron fences walking paths and benches home to the cabildo a local History Museum and an outdoor art colony the square invites you to purchase unique artwork or get a portrait drawn by local artists from here take in the Majestic St Louis Cathedral the oldest cathedral in North America and continue to explore the Charming open a French Market the oldest of its kind in the United States the area seamlessly Blends modern boutiques cocktail bars antique stores and old restaurants you can also opt for a leisurely carriage tour of the surroundings or relax at moonwalk Riverfront Park which offers stunning views of the Mississippi River for music enthusiasts the nearby New Orleans Jazz National Historical Park celebrates the roots and evolution of jazz number five Cafe Dumont yes it's touristy but snagging beignet into Cafe a late from Cafe Dumont is an absolute must for anyone exploring New Orleans established in 1862 and situated at the end of the French market by Jackson Square this iconic spot promises a quint essential Taste of the City Louisiana's official doughnut the original beignet is a delightful deep fried Square generously coated in powdered sugar a culinary tradition dating back to the 17th century when French acadians introduced these fried cakes filled with fruit alongside the Fain beignet Cafe Dand is celebrated for its Cafe olate a harmonious blend of milk coffee and chory sourced from roasted and brewed chory root if you're keen on avoiding the crowds consider sidest stepping the French Quarter location and opting for the cafe dant at Riverwalk mall which offers the same delectable menu without the hectic lines number four National World War II museum you don't have to be a history geek to enjoy the national World War II Museum in New Orleans situated on Magazine Street in the warehouse District this worldclass history museum is a captivating exploration of the war that changed the world opened on June 6 2000 the museum primarily focuses on America's contribution to the Allied Victory featuring a vast collection of over 250,000 artifacts vehicles and aircraft the museum brings the war to life through engaging mediums like film immersive exhibits large scale reconstructions and firstperson oral histories providing a moving and insightful experience highlights include an interactive submarine exhibition a 4D theater and the intense dog tag experience which lets you feel the sensations of going to war adding a unique touch to your visit in November 2023 The Liberation Pavilion expanded the Museum's offerings delving into the Holocaust the war's end and the post-war year exploring social and geopolitical movements while a short visit may not cover the expansive topic thoroughly Taylor yor experience to specific interests or catch a movie presentation this premium museum is one of the city's top attractions for anyone intrigued by history number three Riverboat Cruise a delightful way to explore the city and its surroundings is by embarking on a Scenic Cruise aboard the steamboat Naz along the Majestic Mississippi River since 1823 this historic paddle steamer adorned with a vibrant red paddle since 1975 has transported people and goods up and down the iconic River from the decks of the steamboat nachez guests can soak in breathtaking views of New Orleans while learning about its Rich history the tours aim to transport visitors to another ERA with the captain using a classic handheld megaphone and the air filled with the nostalgic tunes of oldtime Music offering various options such as a dinner Jazz Cruise a daytime Jazz Cruise a Sunday Jazz Brunch cruise and special event cruises the steamboat Naz ensures a memorable 2-hour experience each tour includes a concert a narration of historical facts and an optional meal complemented by a gift shop and bar on board as the calop organ plays during the boat's docking seize this unique opportunity at the steamboat nachez to immerse yourself in history and enjoy a delightful Journey Through Time number two the French Quarter if you eager to savor the authentic flavor of New Orleans start your journey in the Lively French Quarter also known as the Vu Kare the heart and soul of the city this area dating back to the city's origins in 1718 is a National Historic Landmark as you Meander through its narrow cobblestone streets you will discover iconic attractions such as Jackson Square fogner House books and the cabildo the architecture captivates with Baroque iron work on on balconies hanging plants and leafy Courtyards adorned with bubbling fountains while the French Quarter exudes undeniable charm its main draw is Bourbon Street New Orleans Infamous Party Hub however for a more laid-back yet equally vibrant atmosphere venture to Frenchman Street which is slightly less crowded but equally offers a lively experience this trendy cousin to Bourbon Street offers affordable food drinks and exceptional live Street jazz music the street has fantastic venues like the spotted cat Snug Harbor and fava Chic providing an authentic and unpretentious musical experience lastly for a full experience you must visit Royal Street a gem among the bustle in the French Quarter it is one of the oldest streets in the French Quarter just a block from Bourbon Street renowned for its galleries restaurants and curio shops Royal Street exudes historic charm this elegant Urban thorough fair is home to early 19th century Creole tow houses and high-end antique retailers wander along the picturesque stretch between Canal Street and Esplanade Avenue to Marvel at the iron lace balconies and Lush Courtyards whether you choose to meander down Bourbon Street soak in the vibrant atmosphere of Frenchman Street or appreciate the Elegance of Royal Street the French Quarter offers something for everyone your trip to New Orleans wouldn't be complete without exploring this historic neighborhood number one festivals new New Orleans a city renowned for its vibrant culture stands out as a top destination for those seeking a good time the city seizes every opportunity to Revel in its unique offerings hosting many festivals dedicated to various musical styles iconic Foods neighborhoods and cultural influences that Define its character New Orleans boasts dozens of lively festivals from Jazz zido and blue celebrations to honoring beloved dishes like crawfish gumbo and beet when while not fitting the traditional Festival mold Marty gr is the city's most famous celebration transforming the streets into a colorful and chaotic spectacle every February or early March the festivities spanning two weeks leading up to lent include rocket Street parties float fil parades and costumed balls creating an unforgettable experience with vibrant costumes elaborate floats and outstanding music another iconic event is the New Orleans Jazz and Heritage Festival the city's largest annual celebration that showcases the rich music art culture and Heritage of New Orleans with over a dozen stages featuring hundreds of diverse bands the festival offers a wide range of musical genres and a Showcase of Louisiana Arts crafts and cultural experiences JazzFest held at the end of April and the beginning of May is a must attend for music and food enthusiasts featuring iconic dishes like kosan de late pooy and crawfish beet in addition to these other noteworthy festivals include tales of the cocktail Essence Festival Bayou bugalo and Voodoo experience with 130 yearly festivals and celebrations your visit to New Orleans will coincide with at least one of these vibrant and exciting events

videos.transcript: New Orleans is a one-of-a-kind destination that's home to a rich gumbo. A mix of history, culture, food, and music that blends into a kind of unbridled celebration found no place else on Earth. I'll show you how you can make the most of your time in the Big Easy. We'll look at great places to eat, sites to see, things to do, fun ways to move about, and places to stay in the heart of it all. I'll also show you what it's like to arrive from points north on the world famous southbound city of New Orleans. First, a little geography. Founded in 1718, New Orleans, called the Crescent City, sits in a crescent-shaped bend of the mighty Mississippi. The massive twin spans of the Crescent City connection 170 ft above the river, and the almost constant ship traffic below, fed by an incredibly strong current, form a spectacular backdrop for the city skyline. The French Quarter fronts the Mississippi River and borders America's widest street, Canal Street, home to street car and bus lanes and six lanes of traffic. 15 mi to the west is Louisie Armstrong International, the city's airport and Union Station, the site of my Amtrak arrival for this trip to New Orleans, is just a quick Uber or street car ride from all the action along Canal Street and in the French Quarter. First up, where to stay. I get to New Orleans often, and I've stayed at all sorts of hotels and lots of Airbnbs until the city implemented restrictions that wipe most short-term rentals away. On this trip, my home base was the Pelum, a small boutique hotel that's highly rated and relatively affordable. It's not far from the Riverwalk, the Alier's Ferry, the Ottoman Aquarium, Caesar's Casino, and it's right across the street from the Zazzarak House at the corner of Common and Magazine Streets. It's also just a short block from the street cars of Canal Street, and it shares its building with the breakfast hot spot, the Ruby Slipper. I knew I was home when I entered my interior windowless room to the Goldfinger theme playing on the bedside vintage radio. The place feels like old Hollywood movie palace meets Bond super villain lair. And while I'll never be mistaken for a member of the Rat Pack, I kind of felt like it every morning when I woke up in my stylish scene stealing hangover friendly hideaway. For me, searching for the perfect place to stay is part of the fun of travel. And in New Orleans, the home to Super Bowls, conventions, and not to be missed Montigra, there's no shortage of hotel options. There is, however, a real shortage of parking places, hence my arrival by train. And overnight parking at hotels and garages is skyhigh. In New Orleans, you don't need a car. This is a city where you will want to walk. It exudes charm at every turn. From the row iron balconies above to the live music below, it's a sensation for the eyes and the soul. That said, you will want to hop aboard street cars and the Alers Point Ferry. And the easiest way to do that is to get the La Pass app. You can use it to buy 1-day, 3-day, or longer passes for all street cars, buses, and fairies. It's inexpensive, and it couldn't be easier to use. [Music] There are two street car lines. The red street cars run along Canal Street and beside the river, connecting downtown to the Mariny and Frenchman Street. The red line passes by the casino, the aquarium, St. Louis Cathedral and the French Market paralleling the moonwalk, the riverfront prominade and park that's home to monuments and memorials and the riverboat city of New Orleans and its sisterhip, the Steamboat Natches. Both offer jazz cruises and dining options. The Green St. Charles Avenue line travels through Uptown and the Garden District, past Stately Mansions as well as Lyola and two-lane universities. The St. Charles Street car is a New Orleans highlight and shouldn't be missed. You can catch it at Canal and Coranderled streets. As Bourbon Street crosses Canal Street, its name changes to Coranderlet. The only street car drawback, sometimes there's a bit of a wait. Before we hop on the ferry, let's explore the French Quarter, the 400 plus acre historic and cultural heart of New Orleans. Its focal point is the stately St. Louis Cathedral, which overlooks Jackson Square. At the square center is an equestrian statue of Andrew Jackson, who's credited with victory in the 1815 Battle of New Orleans. Iron fences, walkways, benches, and Parisian style landscaping are all from the square's original design, as are the red brick four-story apartment buildings on either side of the square. To the left of the cathedral is the Cabildo, where the Louisiana Purchase was signed. To the right is the Presbyter, now a museum. Artists use the square's fencing to display their works and entertainers of all sorts hold in and around the cathedral. [Music] If the cathedral is open, even if you aren't Catholic or particularly religious, make sure to take a look inside. It's home to beautifully painted ceilings, intricate stained glass windows, and historic artwork. If you're lucky, you might even be treated to a free concert. And not for nothing, the Cathedral's AC system is topnotch in New Orleans, where summers are beyond hot and humid. That truly is a blessing. For visiting sports fans, conventioneers, and people of a certain age, the big draw in New Orleans is Bourbon Street. If you've never been before, it's a lot to take in. restaurants, bars, crowds, live music, and entertainment of all sorts. On Bourbon Street, almost anything goes. Days are calmer than nights, so if it's your first time, you might want to wait in with an afternoon stroll. When the sun sets, it can get wild. And during monograph season, the wildness is amped up almost beyond belief. In this city, you can drink alcoholic beverages while walking on the streets. Restaurants and bars even provide customers with go cups. Even here, public drunkenness is a thing. But you'll have a better chance of spotting a unicorn than seeing an arrest for it. New Orleans is the perfect place for all sorts of fun. But always play it safe. Protect your wallet. Don't walk in isolated areas alone. and realize if strangers come up to you on the street, they want something from you. New Orleans is truly a food lovers paradise. Timeout's 2025 food rankings, named it the best city for food in the world. I'll share a few of my favorite places, but finding your own will be the best part of your visit here. If I'm on Bourbon Street, my go-to spot is the Desire Oyster Bar at the Royal Sinesta Hotel. You usually won't have to wait to get in. I'm partial to the massive roast beef poboy. Just outside the quarter between the riverwalk and the casino at the Hilton Hotel is Drago or Draos at the home of the legendary charroyd oysters. They welcome both pronunciations. That taste sensation started in 1993 when a sauce of garlic, butter, and herbs was brushed on a fresh batch of oysters that were dusted with a blend of Parmesan and Romano cheese and cooked in their shell on a hot grill. There's a lot on the menu at Drago, like alligator tacos, but charroile oysters as a starter or a main course should be required by law. It's first come, first serve here. They don't take reservations, but bar seating is open and service at the bar here is stellar. For dessert, beignetes at Cafe Deond is a requirement, at least once while you're in New Orleans, and there's no excuse for missing it. Cafe Dumont is open 24 hours a day, 7 days a week. It only closes for Christmas and occasionally when a hurricane blows in. Beignetes are the featured attraction. One order consists of three square pieces of fried dough piled high with powdered sugar. And happiness on a plate is as simple as that. You haven't been to New Orleans if you haven't been here. And you haven't been here if your shirt's not covered in powdered sugar. For breakfast, you've got to eat at the Ruby Slipper. There are eight locations spread throughout the city. Fortunately, I didn't have to walk far to indulge in their deliciously special take on the most important meal of the day. You can't stay at the Pelum Hotel and eat anywhere else but here. For me, two days running, it was their eggs benedict that they call Benny's. I had the chicken St. Charles and the eggs cushon, a slowcooked apple braised pork. But there's so much more to love on their menu, which will keep you coming back morning after morning. Hopping on the Alier's Ferry for a quick cruise across the Mississippi is a great way to start or end a day. And again, the Le Pass app is the easiest way to board if you're waffling about the high price of a paddleboat cruise like the city of New Orleans or the Natchez by the cathedral or the Creole Queen not far from the fairies dock by the riverw walk. Do the ferry first. It might check all your river sightseeing boxes. and for just a couple of bucks. From the upper deck, you'll feel the river's incredibly fast current as you see massive tankers and cargo ships speed by. And you'll have great views of the Crescent City Connection and the skyline both during the day and at night. And if you're an Anglophile or sci-fi fan like me, there's a special treat across the river at Alger's Point. Just a short walk from the dock is the Crown and Anchor, an English pub with a cool twist. Here you enter through Doctor Who's Tardis. Once you pass through this big blue British police box, a friendly bartender loaded with grown-inducing jokes serves up just what's needed, who themed specialty drinks. The pub's ceilings and walls are covered in all things British, and the outdoor space is built for lawn game loving crowds. If you're on Algers Point at night, just make sure you know when the last ferry departs so you don't miss your ride back to the bustling heart of New Orleans. Next to the ferry dock is the Ottoman Aquarium, home to more than 3600 animals representing 250 species. Visitors are transported to the underwater worlds of the Caribbean and the Amazon rainforest, and to the waters that give New Orleans its lifeblood, the Mississippi River and the Gulf of Mexico. Here you can pet stingrays in a 13,000galon touch pool. Explore the ecosystem around and under an oil rig in the Gulf teameming with fish. Walk through a glass tunnel surrounded by all sorts of ocean life. [Music] See a white alligator up close in a Louisiana swamp. And spend time with 20 African penguins. [Music] USA Today named Otaban Aquarium one of the top five in the nation. It's also home to the Ottoman Insectarium where crawlies seem less creepy and bug snacks are on the menu. The can't miss here is the butterfly garden, a lush flower-filled paradise that's home to hundreds of freeflying butterflies from around the world. The garden also offers great views of the Mississippi River below. From the aquarium, a quick street car ride down the river, and you're at the French market. Opened in 1791, this New Orleans landmark includes a huge flea market, a farmers market, shops along the colonade, and eeries spread throughout. This is the perfect place to find that quintessential New Orleans souvenir and to people watch in the shade. For more than 200 years, the French market's commerce, community, and culture have been irresistible to visitors and locals alike. On the edge of the French Quarter, eight blocks from the Mississippi, is the mustsea St. Louis Cemetery number one. Established in 1789, this so-called city of the dead is one of three Catholic cemeteries in New Orleans. Most of the graves are above ground vaults, a necessity in a city below sea level with a high water table. Lots of famous New Orleans are buried here, but two grave sites are big tourist draws. The first is believed to be where renowned voodoo priestess Marie Lavo is interred. At her grave, some people leave offerings like coins, beads, candles, and flowers, believing her spirit can grant wishes or protection. And the second popular grave site isn't even occupied, but it's impossible to miss. This 9-ft tall stone pyramid stands out from all the other boxy above ground graves that have been crumbling for more than two centuries. In 2010, actor Nicholas Cage bought two plots here, using one to build this pyramid mausoleum. He doesn't talk about his scene stealing tomb. Could it be an homage to the National Treasure movie franchise? Who knows? To see his future resting place and so many more of the thousand graves in this city of the dead. You'll have to take a day or night tour. The cemetery is closed to the public. From here, let's hop over the central business district to the National World War II Museum. In 2000, it opened as the National D-Day Museum because the boats called Higgins boats used to land Allied forces on Normandy beaches were designed and built here in New Orleans by Higgins Industries. As the museum quickly grew and expanded, the US Congress designated it as the National World War II Museum. Here you'll learn the story of the American experience in the war that changed the world, why it was fought, how it was won, and what it means today. When you tour the museum, set aside at least 3 hours to see it all. Through exhibits, multimedia experiences, and thousands of personal accounts, the museum takes visitors on an unforgettable tour of World War II from Europe to the Pacific and beyond. You can stay at the Art Deco Higgins Hotel for an even more immersive World War II experience. [Music] Now, if you're a train enthusiast like me, this part is for you. I arrived in New Orleans via the southbound city of New Orleans, a train already famous before Arlo Guthrie beautifully told its story in song. If you're coming from Jackson, Memphis, or Chicago, or points in between, this Amtrak train is a wonderful option. My journey south began just over the Louisiana state line in Mcome, Mississippi. From there, it's a mere hour and a half into the city, and once you pass Ponchhatula, Louisiana, it's a spectacular sight-filled journey that really sets the stage for a great time ahead in the Big Easy. Along the way, you'll see closeup what life on the bayus and swamps of southeast Louisiana is really like. Shortly after the train moves over the Manshack Pass, it edges up against the western shore of massive Lake Poncha train. And immediately after it crosses under the I-55 I 10 split, it turns east, paralleling the I 10 interstate and the Bonnie carry spillway as it rapidly makes its way past the airport and into the western edge of the metro area. Riding the city of New Orleans is an easy, stress-free way to get into the city and right downtown without having to maneuver through often heavy traffic. Just make sure to grab a window seat in the observation car when you pass over the Louisiana state line. The scenery does not disappoint. Thank you on train number 59, the world famous southbound city of New Orleans. Once at Union Station, you can take a street car, Uber, or taxi just a few city blocks to your hotel. Soon you'll also be able to arrive in the city via Amtrak's new Migra line which will connect New Orleans to Mobile, Alabama via the Mississippi coastal towns of Bay St. Louis, Gulfport, Buxy and Pasigula. Whether you're here in the heat of summer, at the height of Migra, or during the holiday season, New Orleans surprises, but it never disappoints. If you find this guide helpful, subscribe to my channel. newsocracy for more like it, including guides that focus on the Mississippi GF coast, Hawaii, London, Sydney, and lots of other places. As always, I'm Jim Albrittain. Thanks for watching. [Music]

videos.transcript: 3 days in New Orleans is a celebration of music, food, and history. From powdered sugar mornings at Cafe Du Monde to oak line street car rides, bayou adventures, and nights of jazz and Bourbon Street lights, this itinerary brings the Crescent City to life. Let's dive in. Best time to visit. The best months to experience New Orleans are March to May and October to November when days average around 72 to 82° F 22 to 28° C. During these stretches, the weather is warm but comfortable. The air feels less heavy than in the summer and the city is alive with parades, food festivals, and outdoor concerts. It's the ideal time for walking tours through the French Quarters, garden strolls under the oaks, and long evenings filled with music spilling into the streets. Insider tip. Mardi Gras season is unforgettable but crowded and expensive. If you want the festive atmosphere without the peak prices, late February weekdays are a sweet spot. Airport tips. Flights arrive at Louis Armstrong New Orleans International just 15 miles from downtown. A taxi or ride share will have you in the French Quarter in 20 to 30 minutes for about 30 to 45 dollars while airport shuttles run per person for around 24 dollars. Budget travelers can hop on the city bus for under two dollars though the trip takes closer to an hour. Where to stay. For atmosphere, nothing beats the French Quarter where historic hotels put you right in the heart of the action though most stays here run between 220 to 350 dollars a night. The Warehouse District and CBD are perfect if you want modern comfort near museums with rates usually in the $180 to $280 for something quieter, the Garden District and Magazine District offer boutique inns among leafy streets and grand homes, still only for a short streetcar ride from the quarter with prices around $180 to $280. How to get around? The French Quarter is best explored on foot, but New Orleans streetcars make it easy to reach farther neighborhoods. A single ride is about a dollar and a half, or you can pick up a day pass for $6. Ride shares are easy to find with most trips between central neighborhoods under $15. Parking is limited and expensive, so having a car is rarely worth the hassle unless you're planning day trips. Insider tip. The St. Charles line isn't just transportation. Ride it in daylight to take in oak-lined boulevards and Garden District mansions along the way. Day one, French Quarter icons and jazz nights. Morning, breakfast at Cafe Du Monde. Start your New Orleans journey at Cafe Du Monde, a city institution since 1862. This open-air cafe is famous for its beignet, square powdered sugar pastries, and chicory coffee, a local tradition. A serving of three beignet with coffee or hot chocolate costs around $6 to $8, making it both affordable and iconic. With brass bands often playing nearby and the Mississippi River just steps away, it's the quintessential New Orleans breakfast. Morning, Jackson Square and surroundings. Right across from Cafe Du Monde is Jackson Square, the heart of the French Quarter. The square is framed by historic landmarks, St. Louis Cathedral with its striking spires, the Cabildo where the Louisiana Purchase was signed, and the Presbytere which now houses vibrant Mardi Gras exhibits. Street performers, painters, and jazz musicians gather here daily, turning the square into a living stage. Set aside at least an hour to take in both the architecture and the lively atmosphere. Late morning, Royal, Bourbon, Chartres, and Decatur streets. From Jackson Square, step into the French Quarter's most famous streets, each offering a different side of the city. Royal Street is the most refined, lined with antique shops, art galleries, and wrought-iron balconies draped in flowers. Many afternoons, you'll find brass bands performing on the corners, turning shopping into a street concert. Bourbon Street shows the city's wilder side, buzzing with neon lights, bars, and live music pouring out of nearly every doorway. Even in daylight, it's an experience to walk its length. Chartres Street is quieter, home to some of the Quarter's oldest buildings, including historic townhouses and smaller hidden courtyards that give a sense of colonial New Orleans. And on Decatur Street, the energy shifts again with casual cafes, souvenir shops, and quick bites like po'boys and pralines. Exploring these streets together gives you the full character of the French Quarter, elegant, historic, lively, and sometimes chaotic, but always unforgettable. Insider tip, Royal Street is the best in the late morning before it gets busy, while Bourbon Street is worth returning to after dark if you want to see it at its most vibrant. Afternoon, New Orleans Pharmacy Museum. Before sitting down for lunch, visit the New Orleans Pharmacy Museum, housed in a historic 19th-century apothecary. Inside, you'll find shelves lined with antique bottles, medicinal instruments, and exhibits on the city's early pharmacists, including stories about voodoo potions once sold here. The collection is unusual, but fascinating, offering a different perspective of the city's past. Plan to spend around 45 minutes exploring its rooms and hidden courtyard. Admission is about $10, or you can join one of the museum's guided tours for around $15 to hear detailed stories about the history of medicine in New Orleans. Insider tip: Look for the original glass jars and hand-blown bottles. Many of them still contain their mysterious old remedies. Late afternoon, lunch at Coop's Place. When you're ready to eat, head to Coop's Place, a no-frills favorite known for hearty New Orleans comfort food. Their menu highlights classics like rabbit and sausage jambalaya, seafood gumbo, and fried chicken served in generous portions. It's a casual spot often packed with locals and visitors alike, but worth the wait for authentic flavors. Expect to spend around $15 to $25 per person with a drink. Early evening, French Market. After lunch, stroll through the French Market, a marketplace that has been part of New Orleans' life since the late 1700s. What began as a trading post for French and Spanish colonists has grown into a six-block stretch of food stalls, local crafts, and specialty shops. You can find everything from hot sauces and Cajun spices to pralines and handmade jewelry. Even if you're not shopping, the atmosphere's lively, with musicians often performing along the edges and the smell of Creole dishes filling the air. Evening, dinner at Muriel's Jackson Square. For dinner, settle into Muriel's, one of the French Quarter's most atmospheric restaurants. Overlooking Jackson Square, this elegant spot serves refined Creole cuisine in a historic building dating back to the 1700s. Signature dishes include turtle soup, shrimp and goat cheese crepes, and their popular bread pudding. The interiors are richly decorated, and the restaurant is even rumored to be haunted, adding to its charm. A three-course meal with drinks usually ranges from $45 to $65 per person, making it a memorable dining experience in the heart of the quarter. Evening, Frenchman Street Jazz. End your first day with live music on Frenchman Street, the hub of New Orleans jazz scene. Just a short walk from the quarter, this stretch is lined with intimate clubs where music spills right onto the street. Popular spots include The Spotted Cat Music Club, d.b.a., You Nile, and DBA, each offering a mix of jazz, blues, and funk. There's no cover charge in many venues, though drinks average around $10 to $15. The atmosphere is electric, but more authentic than Bourbon Street, making it the perfect finale for your first day in New Orleans. Insider tip: If you want a true experience, pop into the nightly art market on Frenchman Street before heading into the club. It's filled with handmade crafts and paintings by local artists. Day two, museums, Garden District charm, and haunted legends. Morning, breakfast at Bearcat Cafe in Warehouse District. Begin your second day at Bearcat Cafe, a favorite in the Warehouse District that caters to both indulgent and health-conscious eaters. The menu is split into the good cat and bad cat sections, offering everything from lighter plates like avocado toast and acai bowls to heartier Southern comfort food such as shrimp and grits or fried chicken biscuits. Expect to spend around $15 to $25 per person with coffee. Morning, National World War II Museum. After breakfast, walk a few blocks to the National World War II Museum, widely regarded as one of the best museums in the United States. Its immersive exhibits use films, oral histories, and large-scale artifacts to tell the story of America's role in the war from the beaches of Normandy to the Pacific Islands. Highlights include restored aircraft hanging from the ceiling, a Higgins landing craft built right here in New Orleans, and personal testimonies that bring the scale of the conflict down to individual lives. The interactive galleries are designed to pull you into the history rather than simply display it. Admission here is $32 for adults, and most visitors spend two to three hours here exploring the pavilions. Late morning, Ogden Museum of Southern Art. Directly across the street, the Ogden Museum of Southern Art showcases the rich and diverse culture of the American South. Its collection spans centuries with paintings, photography, and sculpture that capture everything from rolling landscapes and Civil War-era portraits to bold contemporary pieces. Folk art and regional crafts also play a major role, giving voice to traditions that are deeply rooted in the South's identity. In addition to its permanent collection, the museum hosts rotating exhibits that spotlight both established names and emerging voices, ensuring there's always something new to see. Tickets are about $15, and most visitors spend an hour moving through the galleries. Insider tip: If you plan to visit both the World War II Museum and the Ogden, consider buying a combination ticket. It saves a few dollars compared to paying separately. Afternoon, streetcar ride and Garden District walk. Hop aboard the historic St. Charles Avenue streetcar, the oldest continuously operating line in the world. The wooden seats and brass fittings give it a vintage feel, and the ride itself is part of the experience as the streetcar clatters past oak-lined boulevards, universities, and grand homes. The slow pace lets you take in the scenery, making it as much a sightseeing activity as a means of transport. Get off in the Garden District, a neighborhood famous for its 19th-century mansions with cast iron balconies, wrap-around porches, and lush gardens hidden behind wrought iron gates. This is one of the city's most picturesque areas, a sharp contrast to the energy of the French Quarter. While Lafayette Cemetery No. 1 is currently closed to the public, you You still view its ornate tombs and distinctive above-ground vaults from the gates, a reminder of the city's unique burial traditions. A streetcar ticket costs about a dollar and a half, or you can pick up a day pass for $6 if you plan to ride multiple times. Late afternoon, lunch at Commander's Palace. No trip to New Orleans is complete without a meal at Commander's Palace, the city's most famous Creole restaurant, known for dishes like turtle soup, pecan-crusted Gulf fish, and their decadent bread pudding souffle. It's both a culinary and cultural landmark. The service is formal yet welcoming, and the turquoise Victorian building is a neighborhood icon. A two-course lunch with drinks usually runs $35 to $50 per person. Early evening, Magazine Street. After lunch, stroll along Magazine Street, a 6-mile stretch that winds through several of New Orleans' most characterful neighborhoods. The street is lined with boutiques, art galleries, and cafes, but also vintage shops, bookstores, and small bakeries tucked between the storefronts. Each section of the avenue has its own feel, from upscale fashion closer to the Garden District to quirky antique shops and local design studios farther down. With its mix of old architecture and modern storefronts, the street offers a chance to shop, sip a coffee, or simply people-watch in a setting that feels far more local than touristy. The relaxed pace make it an easy counterpoint to the bustle of the French Quarter, offering a glimpse into everyday life in New Orleans. Evening, dinner at Cochon, Warehouse District. Head back to the Warehouse District for dinner at Cochon, a rustic Cajun restaurant that has earned national acclaim. The menu celebrates Southern Louisiana traditions with dishes like wood-fired oysters, house-made boudin, and slow-cooked pork. The atmosphere is casual but polished, and portions are generous. Expect to spend $30 to $45 per person with drinks. Insider tip, the small plates are excellent for sharing. So, order a few to sample a wider variety of Cajun flavors. Day three, bayou adventures and Bourbon Street final. Morning, breakfast at Ruby Slipper. Begin your final day at Ruby Slipper, a New Orleans favorite known for Southern breakfasts with a creative twist. Popular dishes include eggs cochon, a slow-cooked pork with poached eggs and hollandaise, shrimp and grits, and their indulgent banana foster pain perdu. Portions are hearty and with a coffee, expect to spend $15 to $25 per person. Morning, Louisiana bayou tour. After breakfast, head out of the city for a guided boat ride through the Louisiana bayou, one of the state's most iconic landscapes. The bayou is a maze of slow-moving waterways, cypress swamps, and marshlands alive with wildlife. As you glide across the water, you may spot alligators basking on the banks, herons and egrets flying overhead, and turtles sunning themselves on logs. The towering cypress trees draped in Spanish moss create a setting that feels both wild and timeless. Local guides bring the area to life with stories of Cajun culture, fishing traditions, and the importance of the wetlands to Louisiana's history. Depending on the operator, you can choose a slower-paced covered boat or an airboat for a more adventurous ride. Tours usually last two to three hours with prices ranging from $30 to $60. Most departures are within an hour's drive of New Orleans, making this an easy half-day escape into nature before returning to the city. Insider tip, if you prefer fewer crowds, aim for an early morning tour. The wildlife is more active and the temperatures are cooler. Afternoon, St. Louis Cemetery number one. Back in New Orleans, visit St. Louis Cemetery number one, the oldest surviving cemetery in the city dating back to 1789. It's maze of above ground tombs and family vaults reflects both the city's Catholic heritage and its Spanish and French colonial past. The tradition of above ground burials developed because of New Orleans high water table and walking among the elaborate stone work feels like stepping into a city of the dead. Notable burials include the legendary voodoo queen Marie Laveau as well as prominent political figures and local families whose names still shape the city today. Because of preservation efforts, entry is only possible with a licensed guide and tickets are around 25 to $30. Tours last an hour and provide fascinating insights into New Orleans unique burial practices and the stories behind its most famous residents. Insider tip, bring water and a hat if it's sunny. The cemetery has little shade and tours operate rain or shine. Late afternoon, lunch at Cafe Beignet. After the cemetery, stop for a relaxed lunch at Cafe Beignet on Royal Street. While best known for its powdered sugar beignet, this spot also serves a full menu of New Orleans staples from shrimp po'boys and red beans with rice to jambalaya and gumbo. The atmosphere is more laid-back than Cafe Du Monde with live jazz often playing in the courtyard. A typical meal here costs 15 to $25 per person depending on whether you're having a po'boy or drink or a full plate with dessert. Late afternoon, Woldenberg Park. From Royal Street, stroll down to Woldenberg Park, a green stretch along the Mississippi River that connects the French Quarter to the riverfront. The park's paved pathways are lined with sculptures and fountains making it an easy and scenic walk with plenty of photo spots. It's also home to the Steamboat Natchez dock, so you'll often see the historic paddle wheeler letting off steam as it departs for its daily cruises. Public art installations and memorials dot the grounds, including tributes to immigrants and jazz legends tying the park directly to the city's heritage. Locals gather here for exercise, picnics, or to simply enjoy the breeze off the river while street performers add music to the background. It's the perfect place to unwind before your final night out. Early evening, dinner at Galatoire's, Bourbon Street. For your farewell dinner, dine at Galatoire's, a famous New Orleans institution that's been serving French Creole classics since 1905. The menu highlights dishes like crab meat maison, shrimp remoulade, and oysters Rockefeller, all served in an elegant dining room buzzing with energy. Jackets are traditionally required for men in the evening, underscoring the restaurant's refined atmosphere. Dinner with drinks usually ranges from 50 to 80 dollars per person depending on your selections, but the experience is worth it for a true taste of New Orleans tradition. Insider tip, book a reservation well in advance as Galatoire's is one of the city's most in-demand dining spots, especially on weekends. Evening, Bourbon Street nightlife. End your trip on Bourbon Street, New Orleans' most famous strip. By night, it's a whirlwind of neon lights, live music, and packed bars spilling onto the street. From historic jazz clubs to rowdy piano bars, every doorway offers a different soundtrack. Drinks typically run 10 to 15 dollars with plenty of chances to try local favorites like a hurricane or a Sazerac. Even if you're not here for the party scene, a stroll down Bourbon Street after dark is an essential New Orleans experience. Loud, colorful, and unforgettable. Insider tip, for a slightly calmer take on Bourbon Street, visit Lafitte's Blacksmith Shop Bar, one of the oldest bars in America where the candlelit interior feels like stepping back in time. That's our three-day adventure through New Orleans. Powdered sugar mornings, oak-lined boulevards, bayou waters, and nights filled with music. If this trip inspired you, remember to like, subscribe, and hit the bell. And when you're ready for more, watch our New York itinerary, a guide to the city that never sleeps.

videos.title: 10 BEST Things To Do In New Orleans, Louisiana
videos.description:
Your New Orleans guide to the best things to do in the Big Easy! Don't miss checking out these MUST-SEE attractions that you will find in beautiful New Orleans!
